decisions.decision: r > 0 is NOT universal — cos(t)+cos(2t) gives r=-1
decisions.rationale: Commensurate frequencies can destroy peak-gap correlation

decisions.decision: RS-like sums give r ~ 0.13 > 0 regardless of phases
decisions.rationale: 45-sigma significance, N=10 to 500, log frequencies + 1/sqrt(n) amplitudes
From spectral moments m0~logN, m2~(logN)^3/3, m4~(logN)^5/5

decisions.decision: Bridge variance V(g) increasing in core (g < 1.1*g_bar)
decisions.rationale: Verified N=50,200,1000. Oscillates in tail but core has 85%+ mass.

decisions.decision: Proof: r > 0 via bridge monotonicity + Chebyshev inequality
decisions.rationale: E[P|g] = sqrt(2V/pi) increasing in core, Cov(g,P) > 0 by core-tail decomposition

next_action: Make V(g) monotonicity rigorous (item 11), or extend GP result to actual zeta (item 13). The proof is at sketch level — needs either analytic or refined numerical verification of core monotonicity.
context_notes: Session 11b was the 'Ralph Wiggum' deep dive. Key breakthrough: r > 0 can be proved from Gaussian process theory. The RS sum converges to a GP with bounded irregularity index (alpha^2 -> 4/5). For this GP, the bridge variance at the midpoint is increasing in gap length (verified numerically), which via Chebyshev's inequality gives Cov(g,P) > 0. The proof has one technical gap: the core monotonicity of V(g) needs to be made rigorous. Two papers produced: rigidity_exclusion_v3.tex (density bound) and r_positivity_theorem.tex (proof of r > 0).
